questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between SDN and NFV? (4 marks)
keyPoints: SDN separates the network's control plane from the data plane to enable central programmability of network behavior.
NFV decouples network functions from proprietary hardware to run them as software on virtual machines.
They are complementary; SDN controls connectivity, NFV provides virtualized services.

questions.question: Discuss the significant advantages that SDN and NFV offer for managing large-scale IoT networks. Provide specific examples. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Centralized Management & Automation: SDN allows for automated provisioning and policy enforcement across millions of devices, reducing OPEX.
Dynamic Resource Allocation: SDN can reroute traffic based on real-time IoT demand (e.g., prioritizing alarm data).
Cost Efficiency: NFV reduces CAPEX by using COTS servers instead of expensive appliances.
Scalability: NFV allows VNFs (e.g., IoT gateways) to be elastically scaled with device count.
Enhanced Security: SDN's global view enables quick detection and isolation of compromised IoT devices.
Network Slicing: Creating virtual networks for different applications (e.g., one slice for low-latency vehicle communication, another for high-bandwidth video).

questions.question: Explain what 'network slicing' is and how SDN/NFV enable it for IoT. (4 marks)
keyPoints: Network slicing creates multiple virtual, logical networks on shared physical infrastructure.
Each slice is tailored for a specific IoT application with different QoS requirements (latency, bandwidth, reliability).
SDN provides programmable control to define and manage the slices; NFV provides the virtualized functions within each slice.
Example: A smart factory has separate slices for low-latency robotic control vs. best-effort sensor logging.

questions.question: Draw and explain the SDN three-layer architecture. What role do the Northbound and Southbound APIs play? (10 marks)
keyPoints: Application Layer: End-user business applications (IoT management, analytics) that communicate network requirements.
Control Layer: The SDN controller (the brain) that translates app requirements into flow rules and maintains a global network view.
Infrastructure Layer: Physical/virtual networking devices (switches, routers) that forward packets based on controller rules.
Northbound API: Interface between Application and Control layers; allows apps to request network services from the controller.
Southbound API (e.g., OpenFlow): Interface between Control and Infrastructure layers; allows the controller to program switches with forwarding rules.
Importance for IoT: Centralized control enables managing millions of IoT devices, dynamic traffic engineering, and rapid security response.
